Use argparse, the user can specify a key file that will contain a filecrypt-key. Import from `scripts\[sectools.py](http://sectools.py/)` to implement this.
The user can also choose to specify a password, if so, then import `sectools` and use it to generate the filecrypt-key, just for this session.
If no key file nor password is specified, assume unencrypted. Disallow specifying both a key file and a password.
Use argparse, the user can specify a database directory, if not specified, the directory is `~\the-fly\`, make sure this works using `Path.home()` or something like that so it works on all platforms
Download from the device `/list_files.json`
These directories should exist (create if not existing)
- `<db_dir>\raw_rec`
- `<db_dir>\wav`
- `<db_dir>\transcribed`
- `<db_dir>\summarized`

Download (use the `/download_file` url) available files from the device to `<db_dir>\raw_rec` and then decode them into `<db_dir>\wav`, as `*.wav` files. Use `scripts\thefly_audio_decoder.py` but by importing functions. If a `*.rec` file exists on the server but not the corresponding `*.wav` file, perform the download and decode even if the local `*.rec` file exists.
Use argparse, if the user specifies `--clean` then if a file exists in `<db_dir>\raw_rec` , then delete it from the device via the `/delete_file` url.

Use argparse, if the user specifies `--transcribe` then run the result `*.wav` file (only the ones downloaded in this session) through `thefly_transcription.py`  (by function import) to save into `transcribed`. Then run it again through `thefly_summarize.py` (by function import) to save into `summarized`.

The user can specify `--transcribe-file` and specify a path to either a `*.rec` file or a `*.wav` file or a `*.json` file. It intelligently decides what needs to be done. Remember to use python imports instead of process calls. The end results are stored in `<db_dir>\transcribed` and/or `<db_dir>\summarized` as needed. This option can be done even if The-Fly device is not connectable on the network.

The user can also specify `--transcribe-all` and it will see which transcriptions and summarizations are missing via file name from available `*.wav` files, and then perform for all of them. This happens even if there’s no The-Fly device connectable on the network, as it is kind of like a local only task.
agent_reply: - Downloads `/list_files.json`, fetches `.rec` files via `/download_file`, decodes via imported `thefly_audio_decoder`.
- Re-downloads/decodes when the server has `.rec` but local `.wav` is missing.
- `--clean` deletes device files via `/delete_file` once local `raw_rec` exists.
- `--transcribe` processes only WAVs downloaded in that session.
I did not test against a live The-Fly device or call the OpenAI APIs.

user_prompt: we are making a big change
for unencrypted audio recordings, instead of recording to a custom format file with a `*.rec` extension, instead, we will record to a plain `*.wav` file extension.
we will do some things to make it easy to assemble a stereo 16 bit PCM file in real time even though the mic is optionally on or off and the bluetooth data might be unreliable as well.
first, I've already asked you to add a `queueSilence` to the `AudioFifo`. This is useful for the next change
when writing to the bt2file FIFO, if we know that the mic is off, then we insert silence into mic2file automatically.
when writing to the mic2file FIFO, and we see the bt2file FIFO is significantly behind, then we insert some silence to let it catch up.

when writing to the file, we only write if both FIFO has data so that the data can be mixed into stereo. Since the enqueuing mechanism now attempts to keep both FIFOs at the same level, this should work.
When opening the file, since it is a `*.wav` file in this case, we put a placeholder for the RIFF header. When the file is about to be closed, we put in the correct data into the RIFF header
I've made changes myself, make sure I didn't make mistakes. I've placed comments around the code too, to indicate my intentions. Some documentation have been edited but I might've missed some.
Basically, if `BUILD_WITH_SECURITY_LEVEL` is 0, it records to `*.wav` files directly.

Also please make sure that, inside `scripts\thefly_desktop.py`, if the script sees a `*.wav` file instead of a `*.rec` file, it is saved directly to the folder meant for `*.wav` files, skipping the decoding, and if the user asked for it, do the transcription and summarization.
warn me if something is seriously wrong
agent_reply: Implemented the direct `.wav` recording path for `BUILD_WITH_SECURITY_LEVEL == 0`.

- [AudioFileRecorder.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioFileRecorder/AudioFileRecorder.cpp:40) now writes `.wav` files for sec0, including placeholder RIFF headers on open and final RIFF headers/truncation on close.

- [AudioFileRecorder.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioFileRecorder/AudioFileRecorder.cpp:790) now pumps both file FIFOs into stereo PCM: mic left, BT right, with forced stop-drain using silence for a missing side.

- [AudioManager.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/AudioManager.cpp:108) now balances the sec0 file FIFOs with silence: exact match when mic is off, catch-up padding when one side falls behind.

- [thefly_desktop.py](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/scripts/thefly_desktop.py:180) now saves `.wav` files directly into the `wav` folder and skips decoding; downloaded `.wav` files participate in `--transcribe`.
- Updated the recorder/security docs to reflect `.wav` for unencrypted builds and `.rec` for encrypted builds.

I did not find anything seriously wrong after the pass. The one thing still worth watching on device is real timing behavior during long one-sided streams, since that depends on callback cadence and SD write latency, not just compile-time correctness.

user_prompt: # Context from my IDE setup:
## Active file: scripts/thefly_desktop.py
## My request for Codex:
edit `scripts\thefly_audio_decryptor.py` to support both `.rec` and `.fly` encrypted mp3 files now

edit `scripts\thefly_desktop.py` to handle the encountering of all cases of files, so `wav` and `mp3` and `rec` and `fly` files, but these input files can all live in the same sub folder in the database folder. remember to import stuff from `thefly_audio_decryptor` when needed
agent_reply: In [scripts/thefly_audio_decryptor.py](C:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/scripts/thefly_audio_decryptor.py:51), `.fly` now decrypts fixed-size encrypted MP3 chunks straight to `.mp3`, while `.rec` keeps the existing encrypted WAV-to-WAV path. It also exposes shared suffix/output helpers for `.rec`, `.fly`, `.wav`, and `.mp3`.

In [scripts/thefly_desktop.py](C:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/scripts/thefly_desktop.py:33), the database now uses a single `audio` subfolder for all audio cases. Device sync and `--transcribe-file` now handle `.wav`, `.mp3`, `.rec`, and `.fly`; encrypted `.rec` becomes `.wav`, encrypted `.fly` becomes `.mp3`, and transcription works against the decoded/plain audio file without PlaybackView-style type branching.

One note: the local Python env is missing `cryptography`, so I could not run an actual encrypted decrypt sample here. I made desktop crypto loading lazy, so `--help` and plain `.wav/.mp3` workflows no longer fail just because that package is absent.

In [scripts/thefly_audio_decryptor.py](C:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/scripts/thefly_audio_decryptor.py:51), `.fly` now decrypts fixed-size encrypted MP3 chunks straight to `.mp3`, while `.rec` keeps the existing encrypted WAV-to-WAV path. It also exposes shared suffix/output helpers for `.rec`, `.fly`, `.wav`, and `.mp3`.

In [scripts/thefly_desktop.py](C:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/scripts/thefly_desktop.py:33), the database now uses a single `audio` subfolder for all audio cases. Device sync and `--transcribe-file` now handle `.wav`, `.mp3`, `.rec`, and `.fly`; encrypted `.rec` becomes `.wav`, encrypted `.fly` becomes `.mp3`, and transcription works against the decoded/plain audio file without PlaybackView-style type branching.

One note: the local Python env is missing `cryptography`, so I could not run an actual encrypted decrypt sample here. I made desktop crypto loading lazy, so `--help` and plain `.wav/.mp3` workflows no longer fail just because that package is absent.

## Active file: scripts/audio_file_encryptor.py

I want `scripts\audio_file_encryptor.py` to detect input file type, if the input is `wav` then the output is the `rec` format, encrypted. If the input is `mp3` then the output is the encrypted MP3 format with the `fly` extension
the way the key and password are handled stays the same
useful files for you to look at are `lib\AudioFileRecorder\AudioFileRecorder.cpp` and `inc\conf.h`
agent_reply: - Detects input by content as WAV or MP3.
- WAV input now outputs encrypted `.rec` using the existing ffmpeg PCM path.
- MP3 input now outputs encrypted `.fly` chunks using the same AES-GCM key/password handling.
- MP3 chunks use the recorder’s 1152-byte plaintext size and zero-pad the final chunk, matching firmware/decryptor behavior.
- ffmpeg is only required for WAV input now.
- Temp MP3 `.mp3 -> .fly -> .mp3` round-trip, byte-checked with expected zero padding.
- Temp WAV `.wav -> .rec -> .wav/.pcm` round-trip, PCM length/content checked.
I left the pre-existing uncommitted changes in `scripts/thefly_desktop.py` and `src/version.c` untouched.

user_prompt: # Context from my IDE setup:
## Active file: inc/conf.h
## My request for Codex:

hmmmm I did a test, the decryption works, hell, I can "play" it, but a 9 minute song is decoded as 43 minutes and it sounds like... well... basically nothing in the code cares about the sample rate at all, which is probably the core problem
the encoder script needs to actually transcode it into the 64 kbps CBR 16 Khz sample rate stereo 16 bit signed PCM format first, before encrypting
thanks
agent_reply: Fixed the MP3 path in [scripts/audio_file_encryptor.py](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/scripts/audio_file_encryptor.py:209). It now runs MP3 inputs through ffmpeg first and emits recorder-compatible MP3 before encryption:

I verified with a generated 44.1 kHz / 192 kbps stereo MP3: after `.mp3 -> .fly -> decrypted .mp3`, `ffprobe` reported `sample_rate=16000`, `channels=2`, `bit_rate=64000`, and the file starts with raw MP3 frame bytes (`FF F3...`), not metadata.
Pre-existing edits in `scripts/thefly_desktop.py` and `src/version.c` are still untouched.

user_prompt: # Context from my IDE setup:
## Active file: scripts/thefly_audio_decryptor.py
## Active selection of the file:
E
## My request for Codex:
implement `scripts\audio_file_shortener.py`
use argparse, take in a path to an audio file
also allow the user to specify an output file path, if the output file path is not specified, use the input file's directory.
also let the user choose a silence level, but this is optional, use a sensible default
open the file, decode it into a PCM stream, stereo if the file is stereo
analyze the file into a list of silence chunks, each chunk represented by a start and stop time, and can provide a duration
divid the file into big sections, if the file is under 22 minutes, leave it alone, otherwise, find the best way to split the file so that each section is between 12 minutes and 18 minutes.
now we know where the rough split points are, go look around the first split point, within a search window of 2 minutes, find the largest silence period.
The middle of the largest silence is where we split. take the audio before the split, go through it again ensuring all silence periods are 3 seconds or shorter.
save it as "<output file path without extension>.part1.<wav>"
then repeat, saving each processed large section as "<output file path without extension>.part<n>.<wav>"
if a section is shorter than 22 minutes, don't bother dividing it, but still remove silence from it.
This function will either return the original file path if the original file is under 22 minutes and thus need no splitting, or, it returns a list of new file paths.
`scripts\thefly_transcription.py` needs to import this new file and new function, and use it before submitting to the AI model, as the models are rejecting large files.
agent_reply: - Decodes audio to 16-bit PCM WAV, preserving channel count, using WAV directly when possible and `ffmpeg` otherwise.
- Detects silence chunks as `SilenceChunk(start, stop)` with `.duration`.
- Leaves audio alone if it is <= 22 minutes and returns the original path.
- For longer audio, splits into roughly 12-18 minute sections, choosing the midpoint of the largest silence within 2 minutes of each rough split.
- Caps long silence inside each part to 3 seconds.

Transcription now calls `shorten_audio_file()` before submitting audio. If the file is split, it transcribes each part and combines the results into one JSON with combined `text`, adjusted `segments`, and a `parts` record.
Also updated [thefly_summarize.py](</d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/scripts/thefly_summarize.py:107>) so summarizing an audio file uses the same transcription wrapper.
python -m py_compile scripts\audio_file_shortener.py scripts\thefly_transcription.py scripts\thefly_summarize.py scripts\thefly_desktop.py scripts\models.py
I also ran synthetic WAV tests: short audio returned the original path; a 23-minute test file split into two WAV parts and reduced the long silence around the split. No live AI/API calls were made.
- Decodes audio to 16-bit PCM WAV, preserving channel count, using WAV directly when possible and `ffmpeg` otherwise.
- Detects silence chunks as `SilenceChunk(start, stop)` with `.duration`.
- Leaves audio alone if it is <= 22 minutes and returns the original path.
- For longer audio, splits into roughly 12-18 minute sections, choosing the midpoint of the largest silence within 2 minutes of each rough split.
- Caps long silence inside each part to 3 seconds.

Transcription now calls `shorten_audio_file()` before submitting audio. If the file is split, it transcribes each part and combines the results into one JSON with combined `text`, adjusted `segments`, and a `parts` record.
Also updated [thefly_summarize.py](</d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/scripts/thefly_summarize.py:107>) so summarizing an audio file uses the same transcription wrapper.
python -m py_compile scripts\audio_file_shortener.py scripts\thefly_transcription.py scripts\thefly_summarize.py scripts\thefly_desktop.py scripts\models.py
I also ran synthetic WAV tests: short audio returned the original path; a 23-minute test file split into two WAV parts and reduced the long silence around the split. No live AI/API calls were made.

commit_message: python changed to use newer scripts
